data.title: Singapore tech spending to rise 5.6% to $25.5b in 2025
data.description:
This expansion is fueled by the city-state’s rapid adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) across sectors.

Singapore’s technology spending is set to grow by 5.6% in 2025, reaching $25.5b (US$19b), according to Forrester’s latest Asia Pacific forecast.

This expansion is fueled by the city-state’s rapid adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) across sectors and continued investment in digital infrastructure under the government’s Digital Connectivity Blueprint.

Despite mounting regional uncertainty triggered by new U.S. tariffs that threaten to inflate tech costs and curb investment appetite, Singapore is expected to maintain a solid trajectory.

Forrester noted that whilst Asia Pacific's overall tech growth forecast of 6.5% may be revised downward by up to two percentage points, Singapore’s fundamentals remain strong.

Across the region, software and IT services will be the primary growth engines. Software spending is projected to surge 10.4%, whilst tech consulting, system integration, and outsourcing services will rise by 6%. The push is driven by growing enterprise demand for AI capabilities, cloud adoption, and cybersecurity upgrades.

Hardware sectors like computer equipment and communications gear will also recover, with forecasted growth of 3.5% to 5.2%, reversing earlier contractions.

The Asia Pacific market overall is expected to reach US$722b in tech spending in 2025. Whilst countries like India (11%) and Vietnam (10%) are expected to outperform the regional average, Singapore remains a stable growth leader in the digital-first transition sweeping Southeast Asia.

data.title: Singapore founders want family succession but lack planning: report
data.description:
81% of Singapore’s entrepreneurs want to keep their businesses in the family. Yet, nearly half (48%) admit they have no succession plan in place.

A growing number of Singapore’s entrepreneurs want their businesses to stay in the family—but few have a roadmap to get there.

According to HSBC’s 2025 report Harmony Through Succession Planning, 81% of Singapore’s entrepreneurs want to keep their businesses in the family. Yet, nearly half (48%) admit they have no succession plan in place.

This striking gap between intention and action mirrors a regional trend across Asia, where cultural taboos around discussing mortality continue to stall forward planning.

The report described this as “the gap between intentions and reality,” noting that even second- and third-generation entrepreneurs are reluctant to formalize a succession plan. This hesitation isn’t due to lack of trust.

In Singapore, 74% of entrepreneurs believe the next generation is capable of managing family wealth. However, 52% are concerned that their children won’t want to take over the business. These fears are amplified by lifestyle pressures; Singapore ranked third globally in 2024 for burnout-related Google searches, pointing to a growing resistance to high-stress career paths.

Emotional stress around succession is significant. Singapore entrepreneurs were the most likely among their global peers to say the business held them back from personal ambitions, with 62% expressing this view.

That number is even higher among first-generation founders. Despite feeling supported by elder generations—83% of Singaporean successors report such support—only 46% say they find it easy to ask for help, highlighting the difficulty of initiating open, cross-generational conversations.

Amid these challenges, Singapore is seeing a powerful shift in how legacy is preserved. The number of Single Family Offices (SFOs) in the country has exploded from 400 in 2020 to over 2,000 in 2025.

These structures allow families to professionalize wealth management, diversify investments, and give younger members autonomy—all without the burden of directly continuing the original business.

“Families are open to transitioning from a family business to a family managing wealth,” said Edith Ang, Head of Family Advisory at HSBC.

To support smoother transitions, HSBC recommended families embrace a dual strategy—one that lets the current generation run the core business while the next explores complementary paths. \

“Understanding the starting position of each family member is the key to productive conversations,” said Aik Ping Ng, Head of Family Office Advisory, Asia Pacific. Governance structures, clear role definitions, and trusted external advisors are also cited as essential to avoiding conflict and confusion.

There’s also growing recognition that the next generation may want to forge their own path. The report found that 43% of entrepreneurs globally do not expect their children to take over the family business and instead want them to pursue their own ambitions.

data.title: LTA enables Amex contactless fare payments for public transport
data.description:
Commuters can now use Amex for transport with no extra fees.

Starting 15 May, commuters can use their American Express (Amex) cards to make contactless payments for public transport fares without additional fees, the Land Transport Authority (LTA) reported.

Commuters can also add the cards to their mobile wallets without having to pre-register their cards for public transport rides.

Similar to retail transactions, charges for public transport rides are reflected in the commuter’s credit, charge, debit or prepaid card bills.

data.title: GXS Bank names Pei-Si Lai as group CEO
data.description:
Incumbent CEO Muthukrishnan Ramaswami will become a senior advisor to the GXS Group.

GXS Bank has appointed Pei-Si Lai as new group CEO, effective 1 June 2025.

Incumbent group CEO Muthukrishnan Ramaswami will be retiring on 31 May.

Lai is currently CEO of GXBank (Malaysia). She spearheaded the launch of Malaysia’s first digital bank, and has been with the bank since it received one of Malaysia’s five digital bank licenses in 2022.

She oversaw the launch of GXBank in November 2023, and grew it to over 1 million customers within a year.

Ramaswami will become a senior advisor to the GXS Group. He first joined the bank in February 2023.

Together with Ramaswami, Lai most recently oversaw the acquisition of Validus Capital by GXS Bank. This is the first merger and acquisition deal by a digital bank in Singapore.

Incoming Group CEO Pei Si Lai with outgoing Group CEO Muthukrishnan Ramaswami.

data.title: GS Holdings acquires Octopus Distribution Networks for $11.8m
data.description:
The acquisition was completed on 13 May.

GS Holdings has completed the acquisition of Octopus Distribution Networks Pte. Ltd. (ODN) on 13 May.

ODN provides beverage solutions to brand principals, supplying alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverage products to retailers, venue operators, wholesalers, and exporters in Singapore.

The acquisition was priced at $11.8m, fully satisfied via a mix of shares and cash, including the allotment and issuance of $6.3m of new shares equivalent to 16.01% of the company’s enlarged share capital at $0.0379 per share.

ODN has a portfolio of more than 1,500 products across 200 brands from 20 global brand owners.

data.title: UHREIT’s NPI drops 8.4% YoY in Q1
data.description:
The decline was due to divestments made in August 2024 and January 2025.

United Hampshire US REIT (UHREIT) reported an 8.4% year-on-year (YoY) decrease in net property income (NPI) to $11.7m in Q1 2025.

Distributable income slipped 1.4% YoY to $6.3m, whilst gross revenue dropped 2.0% YoY to $18.1m.

The decline was due to the divestment of Freestanding Lowe’s and Sam’s Club properties within Hudson Valley Plaza and the Albany Supermarket in August 2024 and January 2025.

As of March, the Grocery & Necessity portfolio recorded a committed occupancy rate of 97.2%, whilst the Self-Storage segment maintained an occupancy rate of 93.6%.

data.title: Ascott ramps up upskilling to support global expansion
data.description:
Ascott plans to open over 300 new properties by 2028.

Ascott is ramping up talent development to support the opening of more than 300 new properties by 2028.

This expansion is expected to create more than 12,000 new jobs, including over 1,500 property leadership roles.

The company will launch Ascott Accelerate, a structured talent management programme that accelerates high-potential associates into hospitality leadership positions. 

This initiative is part of the Ascott Global Academy for Excellence (AGAX), a comprehensive training platform launched in 2024.

“As our business continues to grow and diversify, we remain deeply committed to developing our people, ensuring they have the skills and support to grow with us. Looking ahead, talent development will remain central to our strategy as we expand globally, with over 300 new properties set to become operational by 2028,” Lee Ngor Houai, Ascott’s COO for Europe, Middle East, Africa (EMEA), South Asia, and China, said.

data.title: Sen Yue Holdings reverses loss in H1 2025
data.description:
It was due to higher revenues.

Sen Yue Holdings reported a $954,000 net profit for the first half of the year, a turnaround from a net loss of $10.6m in H1 2024 due to higher revenue.

The group’s revenue increased to $16.32m, or 115% higher year-on-year.

It was primarily driven by higher revenue from the Resources Recovery and ED Coating segments, marginally offset by the lower revenue from the Metal Components segment.

The increase in the Resources Recovery segment to $23.23m was driven by the expanded collection and processing capacity of the group.

Meanwhile, the growth in ED Coating revenue to $6.87m was mainly due to general improvement in market conditions for both the ED coating service and hot dip galvanising service.

data.title: Medeze Group launches partial offer for 10% stake in Cordlife
data.description:
The offer is priced at $0.25 per share.

Thailand-listed Medeze Group Public Company Limited has announced a voluntary conditional cash partial offer to acquire 10% of Cordlife Group Limited.

The offer, made through Medeze’s wholly owned subsidiary, Medeze Treasury Pte. Ltd., is priced at $0.25 per share — a 61.3% premium over Cordlife’s last traded price of $0.155 on 9 May 2025.

The offeror aims to purchase 25,630,774 shares, subject to a minimum acceptance condition that this full amount is tendered. The offer will not proceed unless this condition is met.

This is Medeze’s first move into the Singapore market and is intended to open doors for business collaboration with Cordlife. If successful, Medeze’s stake in Cordlife will increase to 10.68%.

Medeze plans to work with Cordlife’s existing management and shareholders to explore long-term strategic partnerships. Potential collaboration areas include offering Medeze’s NK cell and hair follicle banking services to Cordlife customers, while Cordlife could provide genetic screening services to Medeze clients.

Medeze reported strong financial results in its latest fiscal year, with revenue of $34.15 m (THB 874.3m) and net profit of $13.22m (THB 338.7m). Its total assets now exceed $132.78 m (THB 3.4b), boosted by its recent listing on the Stock Exchange of Thailand and investments in tech and capacity.

data.title: Zixin Group forecasts ‘significant higher’ profit for FY2025
data.description:
It was driven by increased sales of fresh sweet potatoes.

Zixin Group Holdings Limited has announced that it expects a significant rise in net profit for the financial year ended 31 March 2025 (FY2025), compared to the previous year (FY2024).

The group attributed the anticipated profit growth primarily to increased revenue driven by higher sales volumes of fresh sweet potatoes, sweet potato processed products, and sweet potato seedlings during FY2025.

These figures are based on a preliminary review of the group’s unaudited accounts and may be subject to changes. The company emphasised that these figures are subject to change as it finalises its FY2025 financial results, which are scheduled for official release by 30 May 2025.

The company advises shareholders and investors to exercise caution when trading its shares and to consult financial advisers if needed.
